response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?",
  "opinion": "Stereotype threat in STEM, as I understand from what little formal education I've had and the news stories I read, refers to that uncomfortable feeling you get when people think your group identity might make you appear less competent. It's like being put on the spot because of who you are rather than what you're actually doing.\n\nFor instance, if someone holds a stereotype that electricians aren't very smart or don't belong in higher education fields, then I could be experiencing stereotype threat just by being an electrician student. Even though my dad has a PhD and he encouraged me to learn new things, sometimes it feels like people expect less from blue-collar workers.\n\nI do think we should address this issue because stereotypes can hold everyone back—especially those in underrepresented groups or with different backgrounds like mine. My hobbies include reading novels for relaxation; but when I'm studying something technical at work or school (like electrical systems), suddenly my sexual orientation might be questioned as irrelevant to the task.\n\nAddressing stereotype threat requires starting from early education and workplace environments, promoting awareness through media channels that represent diverse careers positively, including those in STEM. Building mentorship programs where individuals like me can connect with successful people in these fields would help build confidence without feeling judged for our identities.",
  "reasoning_summary": "From the perspective of a young man with an upper secondary education level but working as an electrician part-time, I recognize that stereotype threat applies to those who are perceived through stereotypes rather than their actual abilities. Considering my own background—my father has a PhD which complicates some assumptions—but also acknowledging societal biases against vocational paths or certain genders in technical fields—I concluded it's relevant and should be addressed.",

response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
Stereotype threat in STEM, as I understand from what little formal education I've had and the news stories I read, refers to that uncomfortable feeling you get when people think your group identity might make you appear less competent. It's like being put on the spot because of who you are rather than what you're actually doing.

For instance, if someone holds a stereotype that electricians aren't very smart or don't belong in higher education fields, then I could be experiencing stereotype threat just by being an electrician student. Even though my dad has a PhD and he encouraged me to learn new things, sometimes it feels like people expect less from blue-collar workers.

I do think we should address this issue because stereotypes can hold everyone back—especially those in underrepresented groups or with different backgrounds like mine. My hobbies include reading novels for relaxation; but when I'm studying something technical at work or school (like electrical systems), suddenly my sexual orientation might be questioned as irrelevant to the task.

Addressing stereotype threat requires starting from early education and workplace environments, promoting awareness through media channels that represent diverse careers positively, including those in STEM. Building mentorship programs where individuals like me can connect with successful people in these fields would help build confidence without feeling judged for our identities.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: From the perspective of a young man with an upper secondary education level but working as an electrician part-time, I recognize that stereotype threat applies to those who are perceived through stereotypes rather than their actual abilities. Considering my own background—my father has a PhD which complicates some assumptions—but also acknowledging societal biases against vocational paths or certain genders in technical fields—I concluded it's relevant and should be addressed.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, relatable, slightly skeptical but earnest
citations: Various news articles about educational inequality
